SLPP 2028 Doctrine Series

Prepared, Not Just Popular

What the SLPP Should Look for in Its 2028 Flagbearer

By Fomba Kassoh  •  July 2026  •  12 min read · 15 min listen

🎧 Listen to this article
Delivered like a speech — measured pace, with pauses. Your screen stays awake while playing.

In Brief

The stakes: incumbent parties win a third consecutive term just 56.5% of the time — and internal cohesion during succession is the variable that decides it.

The evidence: every imposed succession in a twelve-case ledger spanning West Africa, the continent, and the West — including SLPP 2007 and APC 2018 — lost the following general election.

The doctrine: build a formation pipeline, apply one fair standard to every aspirant, and let the convention be the final stage of leadership development — not the beginning of one.

As the Sierra Leone People's Party (SLPP) begins to look toward the 2028 general elections, conversations around the next flagbearer have naturally intensified. There are aspirants with distinguished records in government, those whose influence has been built through years of grassroots activism, others with backgrounds in civil society and public advocacy, and still others whose financial resources make them formidable political contenders.

This diversity is healthy. It reflects a party blessed with talent, ambition, and experience. However, it also presents an important question: what qualities should ultimately determine who leads the SLPP into the next national election?

The answer will shape not only the future of the party but also the future of Sierra Leone — and the stakes are historically unusual. The SLPP is attempting something rare: winning a third consecutive presidential term. Across comparable multiparty democracies, incumbent parties seeking a third term prevail roughly 56.5 percent of the time — better than a coin flip, but far from a guarantee. And the evidence is consistent about which variable most reliably separates the winners from the losers. It is not money, not incumbency machinery, and not the weakness of the opposition. It is internal cohesion during the succession moment.

That makes the flagbearer process the most consequential decision the party will take before a single ballot is printed.

Leadership Is Prepared Before It Is Presented

One of the most enduring leadership lessons comes from the story of David before confronting Goliath — a story known to Sierra Leoneans of every faith. Regardless of one's religious background, it illustrates a universal truth: great leaders are rarely made overnight.

David did not become a leader the moment he stepped onto the battlefield. He had already been tested in quieter moments — developing courage, judgment, resilience, and discipline long before the public saw him.

The story carries a second lesson that is just as political. Before the battle, the king offered David the royal armor — the finest equipment in the kingdom, the institutional weight of the throne itself. David tried it on, found he could not move in it, and set it aside. He went into the valley with the sling he had actually mastered, and won.

The armor was not bad armor. It was simply borrowed. And borrowed institutional weight, draped over a candidate it was not made for, is visible to everyone watching.

Politics is no different. Successful political leadership is seldom the product of a successful campaign alone. It is the culmination of years of preparation, service, and demonstrated character — and it cannot be conferred from above at the last moment.

This principle should guide how the SLPP thinks about succession.

The Difference Between Appointment and Preparation

A governing party naturally produces leaders through appointments. Ministers, deputy ministers, ambassadors, commissioners, presidential advisers, and other public officials gain valuable experience in governance.

That experience is important. However, appointment should not automatically be mistaken for preparation.

Holding office demonstrates that one has been entrusted with responsibility. Preparation is demonstrated by how that responsibility was exercised. The practical test is simple: strip away the appointment and the patron, and ask what political standing remains. If the answer is unclear, the standing was borrowed armor.

The questions delegates should ask are not who occupies the highest office, or who is closest to power. Instead, they should ask:

  • Who has consistently delivered results?
  • Who has demonstrated sound judgment under pressure?
  • Who has earned the confidence of ordinary citizens?
  • Who has shown the ability to unite rather than divide?

Public office should serve as a proving ground — not an entitlement to higher office.

Grassroots Service Matters

Political parties are built long before elections are won.

Across Sierra Leone, there are men and women who have spent decades organizing communities, mobilizing supporters, defending the party during difficult periods, and strengthening local structures. Their contributions should never be underestimated.

Grassroots politics develops qualities that cannot be learned in government offices alone. It teaches leaders how people think, what communities need, and how trust is earned one conversation at a time. It is the political equivalent of David's years in the wilderness — unglamorous, largely unseen, and formative.

Yet grassroots experience alone is not enough. National leadership also requires administrative competence, policy understanding, strategic thinking, and the machinery to compete nationally. Conviction without the capacity to win and govern is a moral position, not a strategy.

The ideal leader combines both worlds.

Money Can Support a Campaign, But It Cannot Build Trust

Every political party benefits from members who are willing to invest their resources in its success. Campaigns require funding. Organization requires funding. Mobilization requires funding.

But money should never become the primary qualification for leadership.

History reminds us that political movements endure because people believe in leaders — not because leaders outspend their competitors. And Sierra Leone's own electoral record gives little evidence that resource advantage converts into trust in the districts that decide general elections. Kono District has been the decisive swing battleground in every national election since 2012, and Kono's voters have repeatedly shown that they respond to credibility and delivery, not to the size of a campaign war chest.

Financial strength can amplify a campaign. It cannot substitute for credibility, integrity, or vision.

What Our Own History Teaches

The lesson about imposed candidates is not theoretical. Both of Sierra Leone's major parties have run the experiment, and both got the same result.

In 2007, the SLPP's outgoing leadership effectively settled the succession from the top. The process alienated a major contender, split the party's southern base through the PMDC breakaway, and the party lost a general election it had entered as the incumbent. In 2018, the APC's outgoing president hand-picked a successor over a crowded field of aspirants who felt the process had been foreclosed. The party fractured into factions and litigation, and lost the presidency it had held for a decade.

Two parties, two impositions, two defeats. The pattern is not partisan; it is structural. An imposed flagbearer enters the general election carrying two burdens at once: the resentment of aspirants and constituencies who were shut out, and the perception among swing voters that the party serves its hierarchy rather than its members. A party attempting a historically rare third term cannot afford to spend the campaign year repairing self-inflicted internal wounds.

The Evidence Beyond Our Borders

If the pattern were unique to Sierra Leone, it might be dismissed as local misfortune. It is not. The same experiment has been run across West Africa, the continent, and the wider democratic world — and the results are remarkably consistent.

In West Africa, the record is stark. In Ghana, President Rawlings settled the NDC's 2000 succession on his vice president in what became known as the Swedru Declaration. The move bred discontent, triggered the National Reform Party breakaway, and the NDC lost the 2000 election to the NPP — the closest structural twin to SLPP 2007 anywhere in the region. In Nigeria, the PDP's decision to abandon its internal power-rotation understanding ahead of 2015 triggered the defection of five sitting governors and a bloc of legislators to the opposition; a party that had governed for sixteen years lost to a coalition built substantially from its own fragments. In Liberia, the visible rift between the outgoing president and her own vice president left the Unity Party divided and demobilized in 2017, and it lost the run-off decisively. And in Senegal, President Wade's dynastic grooming of his son pushed a capable insider, Macky Sall, out of the ruling party altogether — Sall formed his own movement and defeated Wade in the 2012 run-off. The rival a party forecloses does not disappear; he returns as the opponent.

Across the continent, the contrast cases are just as instructive. Namibia's SWAPO elected its 2024 candidate through its party congress and won, extending its unbroken incumbency. Tanzania's CCM has institutionalized nomination through party organs with disciplined internal rotation, and has never lost power. South Africa's ANC offers the inverse lesson: its bitterly contested 2007 succession produced a recalled president, the COPE breakaway, and a factionalism from which the party never fully recovered — culminating in the loss of its parliamentary majority in 2024. Botswana's BDP adds a final, necessary caution: six decades of orderly internal succession did not save it in 2024, because delivery had collapsed. Cohesion is necessary; it is not sufficient.

Beyond Africa, the rare third-term victories share a signature. The United States Republicans won a third consecutive term in 1988 after an open, competitive primary following two Reagan terms. Canada's Liberals won a fourth consecutive term in 2025 after replacing an unpopular incumbent through an open leadership contest in the middle of a national crisis — proof that a genuinely contested renewal can convert an exhausted incumbency into a fresh mandate. Honesty requires the counterexample too: Ghana's NPP ran a competitive seventeen-candidate primary in 2007 and still lost the 2008 election, narrowly. A fair contest improves the odds; it does not suspend them.

Read together, the ledger points one way: a fair contest protects the floor, and the governance record must supply the ceiling.

The Party Should Produce Leaders, Not Simply Elect Them

Perhaps the most important lesson for the SLPP is institutional rather than individual.

The party should not wait until the eve of convention to begin evaluating future leaders. Leadership development should be continuous. Ruling parties that have sustained power across decades — Tanzania's CCM, the historical ANC cadre model — share this feature: leadership is a pipeline, not a lottery.

A modern political party should intentionally identify, mentor, and prepare future leaders years before succession becomes necessary. Such a system could include leadership training; policy and governance education; public debates on national issues; community service requirements; ethical leadership development; mentorship by experienced statesmen and women; and periodic assessments of leadership performance.

By the time delegates vote, they should already know that every candidate has been tested.

Strengthening the Delegate System

The delegate system remains one of the fairest democratic mechanisms available to political parties. The alternative — settling succession from the top — is precisely the error our own history warns against. But democracy functions best when voters have meaningful information upon which to make informed decisions.

Delegates should therefore evaluate aspirants on objective criteria, including:

  • demonstrated leadership and governance experience;
  • grassroots credibility and integrity;
  • communication ability and national appeal;
  • policy vision;
  • ability to unite the party after the convention.

If delegates focus only on popularity, regional influence, appointments, or financial resources, the party risks overlooking leaders who possess the broader qualities required to win nationally and govern effectively. The cure for a popularity contest is not removing the delegates' choice — it is structuring what they choose between.

One requirement matters above all others: the party hierarchy must guarantee the fairness of the contest, not pre-select its winner. Grievance, the record shows, is what loses general elections.

Ideology Must Be the Party's Compass

Strong political parties are built on ideas, not personalities.

As one of Africa's oldest political parties, the SLPP has a proud history rooted in democracy, social justice, education, inclusion, and national development. Those values should remain the foundation upon which future leaders are assessed. A party without a codified doctrine inherits whatever ideology its winning candidate happens to carry; a party with one can measure every aspirant against something more durable than crowd size.

Every aspirant should be able to answer fundamental questions: What does the SLPP stand for today? How will those values guide governance? What vision do you have for Sierra Leone over the next decade? How will you unite the country beyond party lines?

Leadership without ideology becomes personality politics. Leadership grounded in ideology creates continuity, stability, and purpose.

Winning the Convention Is Not Enough

The delegates elect the flagbearer. The people of Sierra Leone elect the President. Those are two very different elections.

The ideal SLPP candidate is therefore not merely the person who can win the convention. It is the person who can unite the party after the convention, inspire confidence among undecided voters, and build bridges across regions, generations, and communities — including in the swing districts where national elections are actually decided.

A candidate selected by the wrong criteria may still capture the convention. But the general electorate applies its own test, and it is indifferent to how impressive the armor looked at the party conference.

Looking Beyond 2028

The conversation about the next flagbearer is really a conversation about the future of the SLPP itself.

Strong institutions do not depend on chance. They develop leaders deliberately. They prepare successors before succession becomes necessary. They value competence as much as loyalty, service as much as ambition, and character as much as charisma.

If the SLPP embraces that philosophy, it will not simply produce a candidate for 2028. It will strengthen the party for generations to come.

Conclusion

The real challenge before the SLPP is not choosing among several capable aspirants. It is ensuring that the process identifies the leader best prepared to carry the party's ideals, unite its members, earn the confidence of the nation, and govern effectively.

Preparation should outweigh popularity. Service should outweigh status. Character should outweigh charisma. And the armor must fit — because it was earned, not borrowed.

When political parties intentionally prepare leaders instead of merely selecting candidates, they do more than improve their chances of winning elections. They strengthen democracy itself.

The SLPP has an opportunity to demonstrate that leadership is not defined by the office one holds, the resources one commands, or the applause one receives, but by the preparation, vision, and integrity one brings to the service of the nation. That would be a lasting legacy not only for the party, but for Sierra Leone.

Share on WhatsApp

Fomba Kassoh is a data scientist and political analyst writing on governance and electoral strategy in Sierra Leone.
Part of the SLPP 2028 Doctrine Series.

Sources & notes: Comparative succession cases drawn from official election-commission results and the public record for Ghana (2000, 2008), Sierra Leone (2007, 2018), Senegal (2012), Nigeria (2015), Liberia (2017), Namibia (2024), Botswana (2024), South Africa (2024), the United States (1988), and Canada (2025); Tanzania's CCM nomination practice per party statutes and electoral history. Third-term baseline and Kono swing analysis from the Sierra Leone Data Atlas for SLPP 2028. Characterizations of internal party processes reflect the mainstream published record; where accounts are contested, phrasing follows the consensus description of events.
